text: Hello, I'm sharing a case of a cholecystectomy two weeks after a low anterior resection for rectal cancer with a diverting loop ileostomy. This is actually the second one of these that I've had for the same case within the last month, so I'm not really sure why that's happening, just random bad luck. Just showing how I space my ports trying to avoid the ostomy. I have the camera lateral to the ostomy, so the ostomy is actually just to the screen right of the camera.
The surgeon removes the omentum from the anterior surface of the gallbladder and begins dissection to open the peritoneum at the gallbladder's base.

For as much as I don't like gallbladders, I was pleased that this one didn't present too much of a challenge. Um, other than just being edematous and thick, uh, the planes were relatively well preserved and the dissection followed a, uh, an easy progression and ICG did a really nice job of showing me the ducts.

The patient is a very high BMI. Um, So I did place my ports a little bit above the umbilicus compared to parallel to the umbilicus. But the stoma being in the way and the patient size really didn't impact any difficulty with the case.

The surgeon identifies the cystic duct with the cystic artery positioned above it. A window is created above the artery, and the gallbladder is partially separated from the liver bed to establish the critical view.

The surgeon releases more of the posterior aspect of the gallbladder from the liver bed, achieving a clear critical view with the cystic duct and artery as the only connecting structures. The surgeon then clips the cystic duct.

The surgeon clips the cystic artery and proceeds to detach the gallbladder from the liver bed. The gallbladder appears thickened and inflamed, with minor bleeding observed due to tearing of the liver capsule during the lifting process.
The surgeon inspects the liver bed, achieving hemostasis. The ducts are clearly visible, including the bifurcation of the common duct into the hepatic ducts.
